subtitle: Friday, 19 June 2026
editor_note: Welcome to this edition of our AI digest. This week we look at how government export rules can disrupt access to AI tools, the growing role of AI in scientific and medical research, a new long-task-focused AI model, and fresh public-opinion data on how quickly people feel AI is moving. Together these stories highlight a recurring theme for our work: as AI capabilities expand, so does the need for clear policy, trusted evaluation, and public confidence.

items.title: Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands
items.summary: Anthropic, a major US-based AI company, spent much of the week working to restore access to its newest AI models after the US government abruptly ordered it to cut off access for all foreign nationals — including people based inside the United States and some of the company's own employees. To comply, Anthropic temporarily blocked access to its latest models (referred to as Fable 5 and Mythos 5) for everyone while it sorted out the rules. The situation stemmed from export-control measures that, by many accounts, were unclear and difficult to interpret. This matters because export controls are increasingly being used to govern who can use advanced AI, and sudden enforcement can disrupt access for businesses, researchers, and institutions worldwide. For organisations outside the US that rely on these tools, it is a reminder that access to frontier AI can shift quickly due to government policy decisions.

items.title: A near-autonomous AI chemist improves a challenging reaction in medicinal chemistry
items.summary: OpenAI, together with a company called Molecule.one, demonstrated a 'near-autonomous AI chemist' — an AI system (built on a model referred to as GPT-5.4) that can help design and improve chemical reactions used in drug development. In this case, the AI improved a difficult reaction that is important in medicinal chemistry, the branch of science focused on creating new medicines. The system worked with limited human intervention, suggesting and refining approaches that human chemists then validated. This is notable because it shows AI moving beyond answering questions toward actively contributing to scientific discovery and laboratory work. For workforce and education planning, it signals that AI may increasingly support — and reshape — highly skilled technical roles in research and industry.

items.title: Introducing LifeSciBench
items.summary: OpenAI introduced LifeSciBench, a new test designed to measure how well AI systems handle real-world tasks and decisions in the life sciences, such as biology and medical research. What sets it apart is that it was written and reviewed by domain experts, meaning the questions reflect genuine professional challenges rather than simplified examples. Benchmarks like this are important because they give a more honest picture of what AI can and cannot reliably do in specialised fields, helping prevent overconfidence in the technology. As AI is considered for use in sensitive areas like healthcare and scientific research, rigorous and expert-backed evaluation becomes essential. For institutions, this reflects a broader shift toward demanding clear evidence of AI performance before trusting it with critical work.

items.title: GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks
items.summary: A new AI model called GLM-5.2 was released, with a particular focus on what developers call 'long-horizon tasks' — that is, work that involves many steps carried out over an extended period, rather than a single quick answer. Examples include completing a complex project, following a long set of instructions, or managing a multi-stage process from start to finish. This focus reflects a wider trend in the AI field toward systems that can plan and act more independently over time. The model was shared through Hugging Face, a popular platform where AI tools are published and made available to developers. For non-technical readers, the key takeaway is that AI is increasingly being built not just to respond, but to carry out longer, more involved tasks, which could influence how future workplace tools are designed.

items.title: Two-thirds of Americans think AI is advancing too quickly
items.summary: A new poll from Pew Research found that AI chatbots are now widely used in the United States, with 49 percent of Americans reporting they use them at least occasionally — a sharp rise from 33 percent in 2024. Use of ChatGPT specifically has doubled since 2023, with 44 percent of users reporting they rely on it. At the same time, 63 percent of Americans said they believe the technology is advancing too quickly. This combination is striking: people are adopting AI tools more than ever, yet many remain uneasy about the pace of change. For public-sector institutions, the findings underline the importance of clear communication, responsible adoption, and public engagement to build trust as AI becomes part of everyday life.

items.text: ● DRAFT — for Policy & Strategy review
AI Pulse — Weekly AI News Digest
Friday, 19 June 2026

body.text: Welcome to this edition of our AI digest. This week we look at how government export rules can disrupt access to AI tools, the growing role of AI in scientific and medical research, a new long-task-focused AI model, and fresh public-opinion data on how quickly people feel AI is moving. Together these stories highlight a recurring theme for our work: as AI capabilities expand, so does the need for clear policy, trusted evaluation, and public confidence.

items.text: [1. Anthropic got hit by export rules nobody understands](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/951703/anthropic-shutdown-export-controls)

Anthropic, a major US-based AI company, spent much of the week working to restore access to its newest AI models after the US government abruptly ordered it to cut off access for all foreign nationals — including people based inside the United States and some of the company's own employees. To comply, Anthropic temporarily blocked access to its latest models (referred to as Fable 5 and Mythos 5) for everyone while it sorted out the rules. The situation stemmed from export-control measures that, by many accounts, were unclear and difficult to interpret. This matters because export controls are increasingly being used to govern who can use advanced AI, and sudden enforcement can disrupt access for businesses, researchers, and institutions worldwide. For organisations outside the US that rely on these tools, it is a reminder that access to frontier AI can shift quickly due to government policy decisions.
The Verge — AI · 17 Jun 2026
[2. A near-autonomous AI chemist improves a challenging reaction in medicinal chemistry](https://openai.com/index/ai-chemist-improves-reaction)

OpenAI, together with a company called Molecule.one, demonstrated a 'near-autonomous AI chemist' — an AI system (built on a model referred to as GPT-5.4) that can help design and improve chemical reactions used in drug development. In this case, the AI improved a difficult reaction that is important in medicinal chemistry, the branch of science focused on creating new medicines. The system worked with limited human intervention, suggesting and refining approaches that human chemists then validated. This is notable because it shows AI moving beyond answering questions toward actively contributing to scientific discovery and laboratory work. For workforce and education planning, it signals that AI may increasingly support — and reshape — highly skilled technical roles in research and industry.
OpenAI · 17 Jun 2026
[3. Introducing LifeSciBench](https://openai.com/index/introducing-life-sci-bench)

OpenAI introduced LifeSciBench, a new test designed to measure how well AI systems handle real-world tasks and decisions in the life sciences, such as biology and medical research. What sets it apart is that it was written and reviewed by domain experts, meaning the questions reflect genuine professional challenges rather than simplified examples. Benchmarks like this are important because they give a more honest picture of what AI can and cannot reliably do in specialised fields, helping prevent overconfidence in the technology. As AI is considered for use in sensitive areas like healthcare and scientific research, rigorous and expert-backed evaluation becomes essential. For institutions, this reflects a broader shift toward demanding clear evidence of AI performance before trusting it with critical work.
OpenAI · 17 Jun 2026
[4. GLM-5.2: Built for Long-Horizon Tasks](https://huggingface.co/blog/zai-org/glm-52-blog)

A new AI model called GLM-5.2 was released, with a particular focus on what developers call 'long-horizon tasks' — that is, work that involves many steps carried out over an extended period, rather than a single quick answer. Examples include completing a complex project, following a long set of instructions, or managing a multi-stage process from start to finish. This focus reflects a wider trend in the AI field toward systems that can plan and act more independently over time. The model was shared through Hugging Face, a popular platform where AI tools are published and made available to developers. For non-technical readers, the key takeaway is that AI is increasingly being built not just to respond, but to carry out longer, more involved tasks, which could influence how future workplace tools are designed.
Hugging Face Blog · 17 Jun 2026
[5. Two-thirds of Americans think AI is advancing too quickly](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/951653/pew-research-ai-chatbot-usage-advancing-too-quickly)

A new poll from Pew Research found that AI chatbots are now widely used in the United States, with 49 percent of Americans reporting they use them at least occasionally — a sharp rise from 33 percent in 2024. Use of ChatGPT specifically has doubled since 2023, with 44 percent of users reporting they rely on it. At the same time, 63 percent of Americans said they believe the technology is advancing too quickly. This combination is striking: people are adopting AI tools more than ever, yet many remain uneasy about the pace of change. For public-sector institutions, the findings underline the importance of clear communication, responsible adoption, and public engagement to build trust as AI becomes part of everyday life.
The Verge — AI · 17 Jun 2026

AI Pulse — Weekly AI News Digest
Friday, 19 June 2026
============================================================
